questions.question: The word 'soccer' is often thought of as American — but it actually originated in 19th-century England as slang. From which official name of the sport was it derived?
questions.source_excerpt: The word soccer is an abbreviation of association (from assoc.) and first appeared in English universities in the late 19th century to differentiate association football from rugby football.

questions.question: Liverpool FC's iconic anthem 'You'll Never Walk Alone' wasn't written for football. It comes from which 1945 Rodgers and Hammerstein musical?
questions.source_excerpt: 'You'll Never Walk Alone' is a show tune from the 1945 Rodgers and Hammerstein musical Carousel. It became closely associated with Liverpool F.C. after Gerry and the Pacemakers' 1963 recording was adopted as the club's anthem.

questions.question: Yellow and red cards were introduced to football at the 1970 World Cup. According to referee Ken Aston, who created them, what everyday object inspired the colour scheme?
questions.source_excerpt: Aston later said the idea for the cards came to him as he drove home from a match in 1966 and stopped at a set of traffic lights, with yellow signalling caution and red signalling stop.

questions.question: True or false: The English Premier League is younger than the original Star Wars trilogy.
questions.notes: Claude override (Gemini out): PL founded 1992; original Star Wars trilogy 1977-1983. Wikipedia confirms both dates — PL is unambiguously younger.
questions.source_excerpt: The Premier League was founded as the FA Premier League on 20 February 1992 following the decision of First Division clubs to break away from the Football League.

questions.question: The dramatic Champions League anthem you hear before kickoff was composed by Tony Britten in 1992. He based it on which famous Baroque coronation anthem written by George Frideric Handel?
questions.source_excerpt: The UEFA Champions League Anthem was composed in 1992 by Tony Britten, and is an adaptation of George Frideric Handel's 1727 coronation anthem Zadok the Priest.

questions.question: When the European Cup (now the Champions League) launched in 1955-56, one club won the first five editions in a row — a streak no other club has ever matched. Which club?
questions.source_excerpt: Real Madrid won the first five European Cup tournaments from 1956 to 1960, a feat that has never been equalled.

questions.question: Brazil's iconic Maracanã stadium was completed in time for the 1950 World Cup final between Brazil and Uruguay. The official attendance is recorded at 173,850 — but unofficially, some estimates say how many people were actually in the ground?
questions.notes: Claude override: FIFA + onefootball confirm official 173,850 with widely-cited estimates of 200,000+ including non-paying spectators — the largest crowd ever at a football match.
questions.source_excerpt: The official attendance was 173,850, though estimates including non-paying attendees put the actual number at over 200,000, making it the largest crowd ever at a football match.

questions.question: When Germany famously thrashed Brazil 7-1 in the 2014 World Cup semi-final, something extraordinary happened in the first half. How quickly did Germany score their middle five goals?
questions.notes: Claude override: Wikipedia match report confirms goals at 11', 23', 24', 26', 28', 29' — the middle five came in roughly 6 minutes (23rd-29th). BBC also confirms unprecedented 5-by-half-time pace.
questions.source_excerpt: Germany scored four goals in a span of about six minutes (between the 23rd and 29th minutes), and were 5-0 up after 29 minutes — the most rapid collapse in World Cup knockout history.

questions.question: Pelé is one of the most recognised names in football — but he hated the nickname as a child and never knew what it meant. What is his actual birth name?
questions.source_excerpt: Pelé was born Edson Arantes do Nascimento on 23 October 1940. He stated that he had no idea what 'Pelé' meant, nor did his old friends.

questions.question: Manchester United was founded in 1878 — but not as a football club for the city. It started as a railway-workers' team named after the local depot. What was its original name?
questions.source_excerpt: Manchester United was founded as Newton Heath LYR Football Club in 1878 by the Carriage and Wagon department of the Lancashire and Yorkshire Railway depot at Newton Heath.

questions.question: FIFA finally introduced goal-line technology to the World Cup in 2014. The push to do so was hugely accelerated by which English midfielder's clearly-over-the-line goal that was disallowed against Germany at the 2010 World Cup?
questions.source_excerpt: A turning point came at the 2010 FIFA World Cup when Frank Lampard's shot for England against Germany clearly crossed the line but was not awarded as a goal, prompting FIFA to reverse its long-standing opposition to goal-line technology.

questions.question: The first FIFA World Cup was held in 1930 in Uruguay. Two coincidences made the tiny South American nation an obvious choice — they were the reigning Olympic football champions, and they were celebrating which milestone that year?
questions.notes: Claude override: hypercritic + history.com + footballsgreatest confirm Uruguay was selected because it was celebrating the centenary of independence and was reigning Olympic football champion (1924, 1928).
questions.source_excerpt: Uruguay was chosen as the host of the inaugural FIFA World Cup, as the country was celebrating the centenary of its first constitution and had won the gold medal in football at the 1924 and 1928 Summer Olympics.

questions.question: Glasgow's 'Old Firm' derby between Celtic and Rangers is one of football's most intense rivalries. Beyond geography, the two clubs were historically aligned with which divide?
questions.notes: Claude override: Wikipedia 'Sectarianism in Glasgow' + Stanford + Celtic Wiki all confirm Celtic with Catholic Irish community, Rangers with Protestant/Unionist community.
questions.source_excerpt: The rivalry has historically been fuelled by sectarianism: Celtic with the Catholic Irish immigrant community of Glasgow, and Rangers with the Protestant Scottish establishment.

questions.question: AC Milan, despite being a quintessentially Italian club, was founded in 1899 by two Englishmen — and uses the English spelling of the city's name to this day. What is the city actually called in Italian?
questions.notes: Claude override: footballhistory.org + worldsoccer confirm AC Milan founded 1899 by Englishmen Edwards and Kilpin; club uses Anglicised 'Milan' spelling rather than Italian 'Milano'.
questions.source_excerpt: AC Milan was founded as Milan Foot-Ball and Cricket Club in December 1899 by English expatriates Alfred Edwards and Herbert Kilpin. The club retains the English spelling 'Milan' rather than the Italian 'Milano'.

questions.question: The football term 'hat-trick' for three goals didn't start in football. It originated in which other sport in the 1850s, where a player taking three wickets in three balls was traditionally rewarded with a hat?
questions.source_excerpt: The term hat-trick originated in cricket in 1858, when bowler HH Stephenson took three wickets in three consecutive deliveries and was presented with a hat bought with money raised by a collection.

questions.question: At Euro 2004, the bookmakers' 150-1 outsiders pulled off one of football's greatest tournament shocks — beating the host nation in both the opening match and the final. Which country won?
questions.source_excerpt: Greece, ranked outside the world's top 30 and 150-1 with bookmakers, defeated host nation Portugal 2-1 in the opening match and again 1-0 in the final to win UEFA Euro 2004.

questions.question: At the 2022 FIFA World Cup in Qatar, an African nation reached the semi-finals for the first time in the tournament's 92-year history. Which country?
questions.source_excerpt: At the 2022 FIFA World Cup, Morocco became the first African and Arab nation to reach the semi-finals of a FIFA World Cup, defeating Spain and Portugal in the knockout stage.

questions.question: Brazil entered the 1950 World Cup final at the Maracanã needing only a draw to win the tournament. Uruguay won 2-1 — a national tragedy that became known by what one-word Portuguese name?
questions.notes: Claude override: Wikipedia 'Uruguay v Brazil (1950 FIFA World Cup)' + FIFA + 'El Maracanazo' Wikipedia confirm the 2-1 Uruguay win is universally known as 'Maracanazo'.
questions.source_excerpt: The Maracanazo refers to Uruguay's stunning 2-1 victory over Brazil in the deciding match of the 1950 FIFA World Cup, played at the Maracanã in Rio de Janeiro on 16 July 1950.

questions.question: Italians don't call the sport 'football' — they call it 'calcio', which literally means 'kick'. The name comes from a much older ball game played by which Renaissance city's nobility, who used it to celebrate Carnival in costumed teams?
questions.source_excerpt: Calcio storico fiorentino, also known as calcio in costume, is an early form of football that originated during the Middle Ages in Italy, particularly Florence, where it was played by Renaissance nobility.

questions.question: A 17-year-old Pelé scored a hat-trick in the semi-final and two more in the final to lead Brazil to its first World Cup title in 1958. Which European host nation did he beat in the final?
questions.source_excerpt: Brazil defeated Sweden 5-2 in the 1958 FIFA World Cup Final at the Råsunda Stadium in Stockholm. The 17-year-old Pelé scored twice in the final, including a famous looped volley.

questions.question: At the 1954 World Cup final, the heavily favoured Hungarian 'Mighty Magyars' — unbeaten in 32 matches — were defeated 3-2 in a stunning upset that became known as the 'Miracle of Bern'. Which country pulled off the shock win?
questions.source_excerpt: The 1954 FIFA World Cup Final, also known as the Miracle of Bern, saw West Germany defeat the heavily favoured Hungarian Golden Team 3-2 at the Wankdorf Stadium in Bern, Switzerland.

questions.question: The first football match ever broadcast on television, in September 1937, wasn't a major fixture — it was a friendly between the first team and reserves of which English club, broadcast experimentally by the BBC?
questions.source_excerpt: The first football match televised by the BBC was an Arsenal first team versus reserves practice match on 16 September 1937 at Highbury, broadcast experimentally as part of the early television service.

questions.question: The phrase 'the beautiful game' as a synonym for football was popularised globally by which Brazilian legend's 1977 autobiography?
questions.source_excerpt: The phrase 'the beautiful game' was popularized internationally by Pelé in his 1977 autobiography My Life and the Beautiful Game.

questions.question: True or false: Penalty shootouts were not used to decide knockout matches in the FIFA World Cup until 1982 — before that, drawn matches were settled by a coin toss or replay.
questions.notes: Claude override: olympics.com + Wikipedia 'History of the FIFA World Cup' + FIFA Museum confirm penalty shootouts adopted for 1978 WC but first used at 1982 WC; pre-1982 ties were settled by replay or drawing of lots.
questions.source_excerpt: Penalty shoot-outs were officially adopted at FIFA World Cup tournaments starting in 1982; the first occurred in the semi-final between West Germany and France.
